arch govern triggered THINK via runConduct(), creating boundary confusion: deterministic enforcement and LLM advisory synthesis shared a single command identity. No invariant existed to prevent future rationalization: 'THINK already participates in govern, so it can also…'. Detected by human review, not by the system.

Constitutional split written: Governance Enforcement (deterministic, auditable) vs Governance Analysis (LLM-permitted proposals only). Future architectural target: arch govern / arch reflect as separate commands. Rationalization argument named and refused in the document itself.

The boundary ambiguity between governance enforcement and LLM analysis was not surfaced by any ARCH mechanism. No DriftChecker check, no structural rule, no semantic review caught it. It required human recognition during a reflection session. This is the gap: ARCH does not yet model category errors or ontological drift — it models tasks, decisions, and causal edges, but not 'this boundary is structurally at risk of future misuse.'
